14: veyikane'u ami asher nikra shemi aleihem veyitpallelu viyivakshu fanai viyashuvu midarkeihem hara'im vaani eshma min hashamayim veaeslach lechata'tam vearpeh et artzam: 'If my people who are called by my name humble themselves, and pray and seek my face and turn from their wicked ways, then I will hear from heaven and will forgive their sin and heal their land.' This verse contains the fourfold condition for covenant restoration: humble, pray, seek face, turn from evil. The promise has three parts: hear, forgive, heal. The verse became the central prayer-promise of post-exilic Israel and has been applied by successive generations as the conditions for revival. Its structure is Deuteronomic repentance theology at its most concentrated: the exile is reversible; covenant restoration is possible; the initiative is human repentance, the result is divine forgiveness.
1: ʾēš yārĕḏāh meHaššāmayim (fire came down from the heavens) — the divine acceptance-by-fire pattern: the same event marks Leviticus 9:24 (tabernacle inauguration: ‘fire came out from before YHWH’), Elijah’s Carmel sacrifice (1 Kings 18:38), and David’s threshing-floor offering (1 Chronicles 21:26). kāḇôḏ YHWH māleʾ ʾeT-habBāyit (the glory of YHWH filled the house) — kāḇôḏ (H3519, glory/weight/heaviness) is the term for YHWH’s visible presence: the dense, luminous manifestation of divine being. The same filling occurred at the tabernacle’s completion (Exodus 40:34-35: ‘the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of YHWH filled the tabernacle’). The temple replaces the tabernacle as YHWH’s authorized earthly address.
2: lōʾ yāḵĕlû haKKōhănîm lāḇôʾ ʾel-bêT YHWH — the priests could not enter the LORD’s house. The overwhelming divine presence physically prevents priestly service — not as judgment but as the excess of glory over human capacity. The same occurred at the tabernacle (Exodus 40:35: ‘Moses was not able to enter the tent of meeting because the cloud settled on it and the glory of YHWH filled the tabernacle’). Divine glory is not merely a description but an encountered reality that imposes itself on physical space.
3: wAYYiKRĕʿû ʾaPPAyim ʾarṣāh (bowed faces to the ground/pavement) — the prostration term kāraʿ (H3766, to bow the knee) + ʾappayim (face/nostrils to the ground). Wayyishtaḥăwû (and they worshiped/bowed down) — the Hishtaphel of šāḥāh: the intensive reflexive self-prostration form, the deepest posture of worship. The people’s response to the glory is immediate, embodied, and communal. kî ṭôḇ kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — for he is good, for forever is his steadfast love — the liturgical refrain of Ps 136 (repeated 26×), which is also the refrain of the Levitical song in 1 Chronicles 16:34,41; 2 Chronicles 5:13.
4: wĕhaMMelek wᵉḵol-hāʿām zōḇĕḥîm zeḇaḥ — the king and all the people offered sacrifices. zāḇaḥ (H2076, to sacrifice) and zeḇaḥ (H2077, sacrifice/offering) — the fellowship/peace offering (šĕlāmîm) in contrast to the wholly-consumed ʿôlāh: the zeḇaḥ was shared between God (fat portions burned), priests (specific portions), and worshiper (the rest). The king and all the people sharing in the sacrifice at the temple dedication is the communal meal that seals the covenant ratification.
5: Twenty-two thousand oxen and one hundred twenty thousand sheep — vast numbers marking the scale of the national dedication event. In ANE dedicatory contexts, rulers made large-scale sacrifices to demonstrate lavish devotion; Solomon’s numbers are within the range of ancient Near Eastern royal inscriptions claiming thousands of animals sacrificed at temple dedications. The Chronicler preserves these figures to demonstrate the all-Israel scope of the dedication: this is not merely a royal ceremony but a national covenant-renewal.
6: kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — the liturgical refrain repeated by the Levites with the instruments David had made. This is the haLLēL formula — the refrain of praise that accompanies sacrifice. The Levites with their cymbals, harps, and lyres (mᵉṣiltayim kinnārôT ûnᵉḇālîm) and the priests with their trumpets (ḥăṣôṣrôT) perform the full-orchestra expression of the covenant formula: ḥesed (steadfast love) that endures lĕʿôlām (forever) is the theological foundation of every act of worship.
7: The bronze altar was too small for the vast number of burnt offerings — Solomon qiddēš (consecrated, Piel of qāḏaš) the middle of the outer court for use. The Piel of qāḏaš is the technical term for declaring something holy/set apart for divine use. The improvised use of the outer court reflects the scale exceeding the permanent ritual infrastructure — the exceptional moment (temple dedication) requires extraordinary measures. The court’s temporary consecration for sacrifice anticipates the theological principle that holiness can extend beyond fixed structures when the divine initiative demands it.
8: mibBôʾ ḥămāT ʿad-naḥal miṣrāyim (from Lebo-hamath to the Wadi of Egypt) — the traditional north-to-south boundary of the ideal Israelite territory (Numbers 13:21; 2 Chronicles 34:8; 1 Kings 8:65). The geographic formula marks this as an all-Israel gathering at the fullest territorial extent of the covenant land — the entire covenant community, from northern frontier to southern boundary, is present. This boundary formula signals the Solomonic golden age as the nearest historical approximation to the full covenant-land inheritance.
9: waYYaʿăśû ḥănukkat hamMizBēaḥ šiḇʿat yāmîm wĕhehāḡ šiḇʿat yāmîm — seven days of altar dedication + seven days of the feast. The double seven-day festival before a one-day closing assembly totals fifteen days of celebration. ḥănukkāh (H2598, dedication/inauguration) — the same noun gives Hanukkah its name (the dedication of the rededicated temple in the Maccabean period). ʿaṣeret (closing assembly, H6116) — the solemn assembly on the eighth day mirrors the eighth-day ʿaṣeret of Sukkot/Shemini Atzeret in the liturgical calendar (Leviticus 23:36; Numbers 29:35).
10: šiMĕḥîm wĕṭôḇê lēḇ ʿal-haṭṭôḇāh — joyful and glad of heart for the goodness. The triple joy vocabulary: śāmēaḥ (H8055, to rejoice/be glad), ṭôḇ lēḇ (good of heart), haṭṭôḇāh (the goodness) — all focusing on the quality of ṭôḇ (good). The people’s joy is specifically ‘for the goodness YHWH showed David, Solomon, and Israel his people’ — the joy is covenant-theologically grounded, not merely emotional. lᵉdāwîḏ wĕlišlōmōh wĕlĕyiśrāʾēl ʿammô — the three beneficiaries of covenant goodness named together.
11: waYKallem (he finished/completed them) — Piel of kālāh (H3615), the completion verb. kol-ʾăšer-bāʾ bĕlibBô laʿăśôt — everything that entered his heart to do. The lēḇāḇ (heart) that initiated the temple project (Solomon’s intent in v7) is now credited as the source of its completion. The Chronicler is careful to attribute the temple’s completion to Solomon’s God-given desire (lāʿăśôT, to do/make) rather than merely his administrative competence. waYYaṣlaḥ (he prospered/succeeded, Hiphil of ṣālaḥ) — the Chronicler’s signature evaluation term (cf. 1 Chronicles 22:11,13; 2 Chronicles 29:23).
12: waYYērāʾ YHWH ʾel-šlōmōh baLLāylāh — YHWH appeared to Solomon at night. The nighttime theophany echoes 2 Chronicles 1:7 (Gibeon) and 1 Kings 3:5 (the dream at Gibeon). rāʾāh (H7200) in the Niphal — YHWH allowed himself to be seen/appeared. šāmaʿtî ʾeT-tᵉpillāteḵā (I have heard your prayer) — the simple perfect of hearing, confirming that Solomon’s prayer in 2 Chronicles 6 was received. bāḥartî bammāqôm hazZeh lî lĕḇêT zāḇaḥ — I have chosen this place for myself as a house of sacrifice. bāḥar (H977, choose/elect) + ḇayiT zāḇaḥ (house of sacrifice) — YHWH names the temple explicitly as the designated sacrifice-place, not merely a dwelling.
13: hēn ʾeʿṣōr (behold/if I close) — the first-person conditional introduced by hēn (behold/if) which sets up the three-part disaster series: ʿāṣar haššāmayim (close the sky from rain), ṣiwwīT (command locusts), šalaḥtî (send plague). The three disasters — drought, locust, plague — mirror the plagues of Egypt (reversed: Egypt’s plagues were judgment on Gentiles; here they’re discipline for Israel) and the Deuteronomic curse catalogue. YHWH speaks in first-person ownership of these disasters: he is not absent but actively disciplining through natural processes.
15: ʿattāh ʿênāy yihyû pĕtûḥôT — now my eyes will be open. The perfect response to Solomon’s closing petition (2 Chronicles 6:40: ‘may your eyes be open and your ears attentive’): YHWH mirrors Solomon’s language exactly. ûʾoznay qašûḇôT (and my ears attentive/pricked up) — qāšaḇ (H7181, to be attentive, to give ear) in the Qal passive describes ears set on alert-mode. The divine response to prayer includes both visual attention (eyes open) and auditory attention (ears attentive) — the full sensory metaphor for divine awareness.
16: wĕʿattāh bāḥartî wĕqiddaštî ʾeT-habBayiT hazZeh — I have chosen and consecrated this house. Both verbs in the perfect: the choice and consecration are complete divine acts. qiddēš (Piel of qāḏaš, consecrate) — making something exclusively YHWH’s, set apart from ordinary use. lihyôT šĕmî šām lĕʿôlām (for my name to be there forever) — the eternity of divine name-presence at this house. ûpānay wĕlibbî yihyû šām kol-hayYāmîm — my eyes and my heart will be there all the days. pānîm (face/eyes, H6440) + lēḇ (heart, H3820) paired — the personal divine attentiveness: YHWH promises both oversight (pānîm) and care (lēḇ) directed at this house eternally.
17: wᵉʾim tiTHalleḵ lĕpānay — if you walk before me. hālak lĕpānay (walk before me) is the patriarchal covenant language: YHWH said to Abraham ‘walk before me and be blameless’ (Genesis 17:1). lĕpānay (before my face) — in my presence, under my gaze. kaʾăšer hālaḵ dāwîḏ ʾāḇîḵā (as your father David walked) — David is the standard of covenant walking. The reference to David’s walk (halāḵāh) establishes the benchmark for Solomonic and future Davidic kings: the question is always whether the current king walks as David did.
18: wăhăqîmôTî ʾeT kissēʾ malkûTeḵā — I will establish the throne of your kingdom. qûm (Hiphil, H6965) — to make stand firm, to establish. The recurring kûn/qûm root throughout these chapters (v11: everything was established, wAYĀŠeR; 2 Chronicles 6:10: YHWH has kept, waYYāqem) now appears in the divine promise of dynastic establishment. lōʾ-yikkāreT lᵉḵā ʾîš môšēl bĕyiśrāʾēl — there will not be cut off from you a man ruling in Israel — the unconditional core of the Davidic covenant, here qualified by the conditional of v17-19: the condition is Solomon’s walking before YHWH.
19: wᵉʾim tāšûḇû ûʿăzaḇtem ḥuqqôtay — but if you turn away and abandon my statutes. The counter-condition is introduced with ʾim + future: the conditional nature of the covenant’s blessings. ʿāzaḇ (H5800, to leave/forsake/abandon) is the covenant-disloyalty verb: forsaking YHWH, forsaking the commandments. ûhalaḵtem (and you go) + lăʿăḇōḏ (to serve) + ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm (other gods) — the forbidden alternative covenant loyalty. The trajectory of abandonment: first the statutes (behavioral), then the full defection to other gods (covenantal).
20: wᵉnâšaštî ʾôtām meʿal ʾadmātî — I will uproot them from my land. nāšaš (H5423) or variant nātaš — to pull up/tear out, used for uprooting plants: the agricultural metaphor for exile. ʾadmātî (my land) — YHWH claims ownership of the land of Israel even while Israel inhabits it (Leviticus 25:23: ‘the land is mine; you are strangers and sojourners with me’). wĕhaBBayiT hazZeh ʾăšer qiddaštî lišmî ʾašlîk mēʿal pānāy — this house I consecrated for my name I will cast away from before my face — šālaḵ (throw/cast away) describes the same motion as abandonment: the house devoted to YHWH’s name can be disowned if the covenant is broken.
21: wĕhaBBayiT hazZeh yihyeh ʿelyôn — this house which was so exalted — ʿelyôn (H5945, high/exalted) — the very word that described the temple’s architectural grandeur is now used sarcastically for its ruined state. ʿôḇēr ʿālāyw (everyone who passes by it) + yiššōmēm (will be appalled/desolated) — šāmēm (H8074, to be desolated/appalled/ruined) is the word used for the desolation of Jerusalem in Lamentations (Lamentations 1:4,16; 2 Chronicles 3:11). The temple’s fall from highest magnificence to an object of appalled horror is the ultimate Deuteronomic reversal.
22: ʿāzᵉḇû ʾeT-YHWH ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇôtêhem — they abandoned YHWH the God of their ancestors. The final divine answer to the question ‘Why has YHWH done this?’ uses the same verb ʿāzaḇ (abandon) as v19’s condition: they abandoned YHWH (ʿāzᵉḇû), so YHWH brought this evil upon them. The covenant principle of correspondence: forsaking YHWH results in YHWH forsaking them (cf. 2 Chronicles 15:2: ‘if you abandon him, he will abandon you’). waYYaḥzîqû bĕʾēlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — they clung to other gods — ḥāzaq (grasp, hold tightly) reveals that the problem was not passive drift but active attachment to alternatives.
